mission.title: consul: Duplicate Node IDs after upgrading to 1.2.3
mission.description: Duplicate Node IDs after upgrading to 1.2.3. Requested by 10+ users.

steps.title: Check current consul deployment
steps.description:
Verify your consul version and configuration:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n consul -l app.kubernetes.io/name=consul
helm list -n consul 2>/dev/null || echo "Not installed via Helm"
```
This feature requires a working consul installation.

steps.title: Review consul configuration
steps.description:
Inspect the relevant consul configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all -n consul -l app.kubernetes.io/name=consul
kubectl get configmap -n consul -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=consul
```
After upgrading to 1.2.3 hosts begin reporting the following when registering with the catalog. The node name for the host making the new reservation and the node name for the conflicted always match.

steps.title: Apply the fix for Duplicate Node IDs after upgrading to 1.2.3
steps.description:
`consul leave ; service consul restart` does the trick when we have this issue.

Other way, as we did (for bare-metal servers), compute the node-id with a fixed ID for nodes. We are doing this with the output of dmidecode on our side this way (which is the same way as Consul was supposed to work on
```yaml
[ERR] consul: "Catalog.Register" RPC failed to server 10.246.104.20:8300: rpc error making 
call: failed inserting node: Error while renaming Node ID: "f282db6c-f22d-d183-b078-fb5a821dd7ce": Node name alp-aot-ccm10.mydomain is reserved by node 2450fa9
3-3bae-f22e-3a82-08c4d71e5719 with name alp-aot-ccm10.mydomain
```

steps.title: Verify the feature works
steps.description:
Test that the new capability is working as expected:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n consul -l app.kubernetes.io/name=consul
kubectl get events -n consul --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -10
```
Confirm the feature described in "Duplicate Node IDs after upgrading to 1.2.3" is functioning correctly.

resolution.summary:
`consul leave ; service consul restart` does the trick when we have this issue.

Other way, as we did (for bare-metal servers), compute the node-id with a fixed ID for nodes.

prerequisites.description: A running Kubernetes cluster with consul installed or the issue environment reproducible.
